title: Job 32
subtitle: Elihu Arrives

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — “I Am Young and You Are Old”

heb.paragraph: Elihu means "My God is He" — the most theocentric name in the book. He is young, angry at both Job and the friends, and has waited respectfully until the elders finished. He represents a fourth theological position: neither retribution (friends) nor protest (Job) but divine pedagogy — suffering as God’s teaching tool.

refs.note: "Don’t let anyone look down on you because you are young" — the principle Elihu claims.
"Of making many books there is no end" — the weariness Elihu’s verbosity can provoke.

notes.note: The narrator: Job’s three friends stopped answering because Job was righteous in his own eyes. Then Elihu burned with anger — at Job for justifying himself rather than God, and at the friends for condemning Job without refuting him.

"I am young in years, and you are old; that is why I was fearful. I thought age should speak. But it is the spirit in a person, the breath of the Almighty, that gives them understanding." Elihu challenges the assumption that age equals wisdom.
"I waited while you spoke... but not one of you has proved Job wrong." Elihu claims he can do what the friends couldn’t: actually answer Job’s arguments.
Calvin: Elihu’s point about age and wisdom is valid. The Spirit gives understanding regardless of age. But Elihu will need to demonstrate this with substance, not just assertion.
Calvin: "not one of you has proved Job wrong" is accurate. The friends failed to refute Job because Job’s evidence was stronger than their theology.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite, of the family of Ram. His genealogy is given in unusual detail, suggesting he is a historical figure or is being presented as one.
The NET: the "spirit/breath of Shaddai" parallels the creation account where God breathes life into Adam. Elihu claims the same creative breath gives him insight.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: Elihu’s anger at both parties is his distinctive position. The friends condemned without evidence; Job justified himself without acknowledging God’s right to test. Elihu thinks both sides are wrong.
Clines: "the breath of the Almighty gives understanding" — Elihu claims inspiration, not just experience. He appeals to the Spirit rather than tradition or observation.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Elihu is an uninvited advocate who enters the court after both prosecution and defence have rested. His role is ambiguous: friend of the court? New prosecutor? Bridge to the judge’s ruling?. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Elihu appears without introduction in the prologue and disappears without mention in the epilogue. God never endorses or rebukes him. Scholars debate whether he is a later addition, an intentional bridge between the dialogue and the whirlwind, or both. His four speeches (ch 32–37) share some theology with God’s whirlwind speeches but lack their power.

sections.header: Verses 15–22 — “I Must Speak or I Will Burst”

heb.paragraph: "I too will have my say; I too will tell what I know. For I am full of words, and the spirit within me compels me" (vv.17–18). Elihu’s self-introduction is enthusiastic to the point of comedy. He describes himself as a wineskin about to burst (v.19). The image is vivid and slightly ridiculous — deliberately so? Scholars disagree.

refs.note: "His word is in my heart like a fire, a fire shut up in my bones" — the same compulsion to speak that Elihu describes.

notes.note: "I too will have my say. I am full of words; the spirit within me compels me. Inside I am like bottled-up wine, like new wineskins ready to burst." Elihu cannot contain himself. The energy is genuine, even if the self-awareness is limited.
"I will show no partiality. If I were to flatter, my Maker would soon take me away." Elihu pledges fairness and claims divine accountability for his words.
Calvin: the compulsion to speak can be genuine prophetic impulse or mere youthful enthusiasm. Elihu may have both. His theology will be partly right and partly overconfident.
Calvin: the refusal to flatter is commendable but also self-serving. Elihu establishes his credentials before delivering his message.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "new wineskins ready to burst" — new wine in new skins generates gas as it ferments. Without release, the skin ruptures. Elihu is fermenting with unspoken words.
The NET: the claim of divine accountability ("my Maker would take me away") elevates Elihu’s speech from advice to claimed prophecy.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the wineskin image is either passionate sincerity or pompous self-importance. Clines leans toward deliberate comedy: the narrator presents Elihu as a young man who thinks more highly of his own words than they deserve.
Clines: the promise not to flatter is followed by four chapters of unsolicited advice. The gap between promise and delivery is part of Elihu’s characterisation.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Elihu positions himself as an impartial voice. He is neither prosecution nor defence but claims to speak from the Spirit. His actual speeches will partially support both sides. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Elihu promises impartiality: he will not flatter anyone or use titles (v.21–22). This is a young man’s boldness. Whether it is also wisdom remains to be seen. His speeches will contain genuine theological insights mixed with considerable verbosity.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBut Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, became very angry with Job for justifying himself rather than God.ESVThen Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, burned with anger. He burned with anger at Job because he justified himself rather than God.NIVSo Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite said: “I am young in years, and you are old; that is why I was fearful, not daring to tell you what I know.ESVAnd Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said: “I am young in years, and you are aged; therefore I was timid and afraid to declare my opinion to you.

rec.text: "The narrator: Job’s three friends stopped answering because Job was righteous in his own eyes. Then Elihu burned with anger — at Job for justifying himself rather than God, and at the friends for cond…"

rows.label: The Angry Young Man — 32:1–14
rows.text: Elihu waited, listened, and concluded both sides were wrong. Age does not equal wisdom.

rows.label: About to Burst — 32:15–22
rows.text: Like a wineskin ready to pop. Compelled to speak. No flattery.

hebtext.note: Elihu means "My God is He" — the most theocentric name in the book. He is young, angry at both Job and the friends, and has waited respectfully until the elders finished. He represents a fourth theological position: neither retribution (friends) nor protest (Job) but divine pedagogy — suffering as God’s teaching tool.

"I too will have my say; I too will tell what I know. For I am full of words, and the spirit within me compels me" (vv.17–18). Elihu’s self-introduction is enthusiastic to the point of comedy. He describes himself as a wineskin about to burst (v.19). The image is vivid and slightly ridiculous — deliberately so? Scholars disagree.

thread.text: "Don’t let anyone look down on you because you are young" — the principle Elihu claims.
"Of making many books there is no end" — the weariness Elihu’s verbosity can provoke.
"His word is in my heart like a fire, a fire shut up in my bones" — the same compulsion to speak that Elihu describes.

themes.note: Prophecy rises: Elihu claims the Spirit compels him. Justice: he promises to address what the friends couldn’t.

prayer_prompt: As you consider young Elihu's anger at both Job and his friends — frustrated that no one has answered Job adequately — consider asking God whether there are moments when a fresh perspective is needed, and whether youth disqualifies or qualifies someone to speak.
